red_flags_for_attorneys: Medication was in checked bag and caused physical harm | Airline refused to pay within DOT limits | Willful misconduct by airline (bypasses DOT cap) | High-value items were properly declared
reportability: File Property Irregularity Report (PIR) BEFORE leaving baggage claim area — this is mandatory for all claims
secondary_airport_zones: Customs inspection areas (international) | Oversized baggage areas | Airline ground transfer areas
secondary_liable_parties: Travel insurance company (if coverage denied) | Connecting airline
specific_floor_materials: N/A — baggage loss is operational, not physical hazard
statute_of_limitations_note: 21 days from delivery for damage claims; 2 years under Montreal Convention for international.
terminal_zones_ranked: Connection baggage transfer areas (highest loss risk) | Check-in counters (misrouting at check-in) | International customs (held bags) | Oversized baggage areas (items left behind) | Baggage claim (theft from carousels)
venue_specific_hazards: tight_connections: Bags not transferred during short connection windows || system_outages: IT failures causing misrouting of bags || damage: Overloaded belts causing rough handling and bag damage || international_customs: Bags held in customs causing delays || medication: Bags with critical medications checked in error and lost
victim_demographics: Connecting passengers | International travelers | Passengers traveling with medical equipment | Business travelers with essential presentation materials

airport_businesses_involved: Enterprise Rent-A-Car | National Car Rental | Alamo Rent A Car | Hertz Corporation | Dollar Rent A Car | Thrifty Car Rental | Avis Car Rental | Budget Car Rental | Sixt Rent a Car | Fox Rent A Car | Payless Car Rental | Advantage Rent A Car | Turo (peer-to-peer at airports) | Consolidated Rental Car Facilities (CRCFs) | Parking companies managing rental return areas
airport_size_note: On-airport rental car operations at most commercial airports — consolidated rental car facilities at large hubs, individual counters at smaller airports
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 100-400+ rental car-related incidents per year at major hub airports
business_categories: Major rental car chains | Budget rental car brands | Peer-to-peer rental | Consolidated rental car facility operators | Vehicle maintenance contractors for rental fleets
cleaning_contractor_types: Rental car company own maintenance staff | CRCF facility management | Airport authority for common CRCF areas
cms_content_angles: Rental car accident at [airport_name] | Defective rental car injury from [airport_name] | Rental car shuttle accident at [airport_name] — who is liable
common_injuries: Vehicle accident injuries from defective car | Shuttle bus whiplash and fractures | Slip and fall in rental facility | Carbon monoxide exposure in enclosed garages
compensable_damages: Medical bills | Lost wages | Pain and suffering | Vehicle damage | Future care
contributing_environmental_factors: Oil and fuel spills on garage floors | Defective vehicles with unresolved recall | Rushed pre-rental inspection | Night returns with poor garage lighting
danger_note: Defective rental vehicles and shuttle bus accidents are the most dangerous incidents
description: Injuries at on-airport rental car facilities including shuttle accidents, defective rental vehicles, garage injuries, and counter area incidents.
evidence_notes: Check the NHTSA recall database for the rental vehicle VIN immediately. Renting a vehicle with an open safety recall creates strong liability for the rental company.
factors_increasing_value: Open safety recall on vehicle at time of rental | Shuttle bus severe accident | Permanent injury | Rental company knew of defect
frequency_note: On-airport rental car centers process millions of customers annually — shuttle and facility incidents occur regularly
high_risk_times: High-volume travel periods with rushed vehicle turnarounds | After long flights when customers are tired and rush | Night returns in poorly lit garage areas
insurance_claim_type: Product liability (defective vehicle) / premises liability / commercial auto
insurance_types_involved: Commercial auto liability | General liability | Product liability | Rental company umbrella policies
key_evidence: Rental agreement (proof of customer status) | Vehicle inspection report | Recall database check on rented vehicle VIN | Shuttle incident report | Surveillance footage | Medical records
liability_notes: Rental car companies have faced major litigation for renting vehicles with open safety recalls. The Graves Amendment limits some rental company liability for driver negligence, but not for vehicle defects or facility hazards.
liable_party_defenses: Vehicle met safety standards at rental | Graves Amendment limits liability | Driver contributed to accident
local_authority_involved: Rental Car Company / NHTSA / Airport Authority / Local Police
long_term_complications: Permanent injury from vehicle defect accident | Chronic pain from shuttle accident
passenger_volume_note: Rental car peak periods follow flight arrival patterns — high-volume arrivals create rushed turnarounds and defect screening gaps
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Check open recall on specific VIN | Obtain rental company's maintenance records for vehicle | Show Graves Amendment does not apply (vehicle defect, not driver negligence)
primary_airport_zones: Rental car counter areas | Rental car garages (Consolidated Rental Facilities) | Shuttle stops between terminal and rental center | Vehicle return fuel stations | Vehicle inspection areas
primary_liable_parties: Rental car company (for vehicle defects and facility conditions) | Rental car shuttle operator | Airport authority (for consolidated rental facility)
red_flags_for_attorneys: Rented vehicle had an open NHTSA recall at time of rental | Rental company knew of defect from prior return | Shuttle driver had prior traffic violations | Garage had documented lighting failures
reportability: For vehicle defect: check NHTSA recall database immediately for your VIN. For shuttle/facility: report to rental company manager and airport authority
secondary_airport_zones: Quick turnaround washing/fueling areas | Rental car parking lots | Shuttle bus boarding zones
secondary_liable_parties: Vehicle manufacturer (defect claims) | Maintenance contractor for rental fleet
specific_floor_materials: Rental garage concrete (often oil-stained) | Fuel return station asphalt | Counter area tile
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-3 years for personal injury; product liability may have different periods.
terminal_zones_ranked: Consolidated rental car facility (CRCF) garage | Rental car shuttle to/from terminal | Vehicle inspection area | Fuel return stations | Counter area
typical_recovery_time: Weeks to years depending on defect severity
venue_specific_hazards: defective_vehicle: Rental vehicles with known defects not disclosed to customer || shuttle_bus: Rental company shuttle with rapid braking or poor maintenance || garage_slip: Wet floors in covered rental car return garages || fuel_station: Slippery surfaces at fuel return stations || counter_area: Crowded counter areas with luggage trip hazards
victim_demographics: Road-trip vacationers with defective vehicles | Business travelers on shuttle buses | Elderly passengers navigating unfamiliar garages | Anyone renting a vehicle with an open recall

airport_businesses_involved: Zoll Medical Corporation (AED manufacturer) | Philips Healthcare (AED manufacturer) | Cardiac Science (AED manufacturer) | Defibtech (AED manufacturer) | MedAire (airline medical services) | SkyService First Aid (airport medical) | AED Program Management companies | ABM Industries (facility management with AED programs) | Airport medical clinic operators | EMT contractors for airports
airport_size_note: AED availability requirements apply to all commercial airports — but smaller airports often have fewer units and less trained staff per square foot
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: Hundreds of cardiac events per year at major hubs — AED use rate is the key metric
business_categories: AED manufacturers | Airport medical clinic operators | First responder training companies | Facility management companies with medical response duties | EMT contractors
cleaning_contractor_types: ABM Industries manages AED programs at some airports | Airport authority medical staff | EMT contractor companies
cms_content_angles: Heart attack at [airport_name] — was the airport negligent | No AED available at [airport_name] caused death | Airport failed to respond to medical emergency at [airport_name]
common_injuries: Death or brain damage from cardiac arrest without timely AED | Worsened stroke outcomes from delayed response | Fall injuries during medical episode | Medication complications from prolonged wait
compensable_damages: Medical bills | Wrongful death damages | Lost future income | Pain and suffering | Loss of companionship (wrongful death)
contributing_environmental_factors: Stress of travel triggering cardiac events | Elderly traveler demographic | Altitude at certain airports (Denver, Salt Lake, Albuquerque) | Heat in summer terminal crowding | DVT from long flights
danger_note: Every minute without defibrillation reduces cardiac arrest survival by 10% — inadequate AED placement at airports is literally life or death
description: Claims when airports fail to respond adequately to medical emergencies including unavailable defibrillators, untrained first responders, or delays in calling emergency services.
evidence_notes: Wrongful death cases require immediate action. Airport emergency response logs are the most critical evidence and may be altered. Retain counsel immediately.
factors_increasing_value: AED unavailable within required distance | No trained staff available | Prior complaints about response gaps | Younger victim with dependents
frequency_note: Medical emergencies occur at major airports daily — response time and AED availability are critical and frequently inadequate
high_risk_times: Peak travel periods with elderly passengers | During heat events | Long international flights landing (DVT risk)
insurance_types_involved: General liability | Medical malpractice (for medical contractors) | Premises liability
key_evidence: AED placement records and maps | Staff training records | Emergency response time logs | 911 call records | Airport emergency protocol documents | Medical records
liability_notes: Most states have Good Samaritan laws protecting bystanders but NOT establishments — airports have a duty to have AEDs and trained staff.
liable_party_defenses: AED placement met FAA/local requirements | Staff responded appropriately given available resources | Pre-existing condition was primary cause
local_authority_involved: Airport Authority / Airport Medical Services / Local EMS / Medical Examiner (if fatal)
long_term_complications: Permanent brain damage | Wrongful death | Permanent disability from delayed cardiac response
passenger_volume_note: Higher passenger volume = more cardiac events per year — but also should mean more AEDs and trained staff. Failure to scale is the key liability
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Obtain AED placement records to show gaps | Calculate response time against survival probability charts | Show prior complaints about inadequate AED coverage
primary_airport_zones: Terminal gates (furthest from AEDs) | Security checkpoints | Restaurants and food courts | Restrooms | Remote terminal sections
primary_liable_parties: Airport authority (for AED placement and staff training) | Terminal management company | First response contractor
red_flags_for_attorneys: Nearest AED was more than 3 minutes away | Airport staff did not know AED location | AED was present but non-functional | Response time exceeded 4 minutes to first shock
reportability: CALL 911 IMMEDIATELY — every second counts. Family should preserve all airport emergency response records
secondary_airport_zones: Parking garages | Shuttle waiting areas | International arrival halls | Long queuing areas
secondary_liable_parties: AED manufacturer (if device failed) | Specific employee who failed to respond
specific_floor_materials: Hard terminal tile (fall risk during medical episode)
statute_of_limitations_note: Wrongful death: 1-3 years (varies by state). Preserve evidence immediately.
terminal_zones_ranked: Remote gate areas furthest from AEDs | Long international concourses | Restrooms (AEDs often not inside) | Parking garages (often no AEDs) | Food courts (staff may not know AED location)
venue_specific_hazards: aed_placement: AED units more than 90 seconds from any point in terminal || staff_training: Untrained staff unable to operate AED or perform CPR || response_time: Delayed emergency services call allowing condition to worsen || communication: Gate agents unaware of AED location or emergency protocol || airport_clinic: Airport medical clinic not adequately staffed during peak hours
victim_demographics: Elderly passengers (highest cardiac risk) | Passengers with known heart conditions | Stress-prone business travelers | Passengers arriving from long international flights (DVT)

airport_businesses_involved: American Airlines (UM program) | Delta Air Lines (UM program) | United Airlines (UM program) | Southwest Airlines (UM program) | Alaska Airlines (UM program) | JetBlue (UM program) | Frontier Airlines (UM program) | Spirit Airlines (UM program) | Unaccompanied minor service contractors | Gate agents (airline employed) | Customer service staff
airport_size_note: Unaccompanied minor incidents occur at all airports — connection airports are highest risk due to transfers between airline staff
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: Hundreds of UM service failures per year — most go unreported
business_categories: Airlines (all offer UM programs) | Airline ground service staff | Airline customer service contractors
cleaning_contractor_types: Not applicable
cms_content_angles: Child left alone at [airport_name] — airline negligence | Unaccompanied minor harmed at [airport_name] | Airline lost my child at [airport_name] — legal options
common_injuries: Physical harm from lack of supervision | Psychological trauma | Wrongful contact with strangers | Injury during unsupervised periods | Emotional distress from wrongful separation
compensable_damages: Medical bills (psychological treatment) | Pain and suffering | Parental loss of consortium | Future therapy costs
contributing_environmental_factors: Staff shortages during holiday peaks | Multiple simultaneous UM requests | Flight cancellations disrupting UM handling plans | Communication failure between origin and destination airline staff
danger_note: Harm to an unaccompanied child is treated with the highest level of legal seriousness; airlines face significant liability exposure
description: Claims arising from harm to unaccompanied minors traveling alone when airline or airport staff fail to properly supervise, transfer, or protect children during travel.
evidence_notes: Statute of limitations is often tolled until the child turns 18 — claims can be filed years later. Preserve all communication with the airline.
factors_increasing_value: Wrongful release to stranger | Physical harm occurred | Overnight stranding without parent contact | Permanent psychological injury documented
frequency_note: Over 2 million children travel unaccompanied annually — incidents are rare but receive significant media and legal attention
high_risk_times: Holiday peak travel with high UM volume | Understaffed periods | Disrupted operations requiring connections
insurance_types_involved: Aviation liability | General liability
key_evidence: UM service booking records | Staff assignment logs | Surveillance footage throughout child's journey | Airline communication records with parents | Child's account (obtained with care)
legal_standard: Negligence / in loco parentis duty of care
liability_notes: Airlines charge fees for UM service and accept explicit duty of care for the child. Breach of this duty is treated seriously by courts. Statute of limitations typically tolled until child turns 18.
liable_party_defenses: Staff followed UM protocol | Incident was caused by unforeseen disruption | Parents failed to follow UM booking requirements
local_authority_involved: Airline / Airport Authority / Local Police / Child Protective Services (if applicable)
long_term_complications: PTSD | Fear of travel | Trust and attachment issues from trauma
passenger_volume_note: Peak holiday travel and summer travel have highest UM volume — when airlines are busiest, UM supervision is most likely to be inadequate
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Show protocol was not followed | Document airline's acknowledgment of failure | Calculate full scope of psychological harm with expert
primary_airport_zones: Gate areas | Terminal corridors between connections | Airline special assistance waiting areas | Boarding areas | Airline club rooms used for UM holding
primary_liable_parties: Airline (primary duty of care for UM) | Gate agent (individual negligence) | Airline contractor managing UM services
red_flags_for_attorneys: Child was released to wrong adult | Child was left unattended for 1+ hours | Overnight cancellation without parent notification | Physical harm occurred during unsupervised period
reportability: Document everything — airline communications, staffing records. Statute of limitations tolled until child is 18 so act methodically
secondary_airport_zones: Baggage claim (when UM and parent separated) | Ground transportation (wrong pickup)
secondary_liable_parties: Airport authority (for unsafe conditions)
specific_floor_materials: N/A — supervision failure, not physical hazard
statute_of_limitations_note: Often tolled until minor turns 18 — claims can be filed years after incident.
terminal_zones_ranked: Gate areas (supervision handoffs) | Connection corridors (child between flights) | Special assistance holding rooms | Ground transportation pickup area | Baggage claim (parent-child reunion gone wrong)
venue_specific_hazards: missed_connection: Child stranded at connecting airport without supervision || wrong_handler: Child released to wrong adult at destination || unsupervised_waiting: Child left unattended in gate area during delay || overnight_stranding: Child kept at airport overnight due to cancellation without parent notification || predatory_contact: Child exposed to unsafe adults due to inadequate supervision
victim_demographics: Children ages 5-17 traveling alone | Children of divorced parents (common UM demographic) | International children connecting through US airports | Children with special needs requiring extra supervision

accident_name: Wet Weather & Outdoor Slip and Fall
airport_businesses_involved: Snow removal contractors (various local) | Facility management companies (ABM, AECOM, Aramark) | Salting/de-icing service contractors | Weather monitoring services (DTN, WSI) | Parking operators (SP+, LAZ) for lot areas | Airline ground operations (for aircraft boarding areas) | Airport authority facilities department
airport_size_note: Highest risk at northern airports (Chicago, Boston, Minneapolis, Denver) in winter — all coastal airports face rain-related incidents year-round
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: 100-400+ weather-related slip incidents per year at northern hub airports during winter
business_categories: Snow and ice removal contractors | Facility management firms | Weather monitoring services | De-icing chemical suppliers | Parking operators
cleaning_contractor_types: Snow removal contractors (local specialty firms) | Facility management (ABM, Aramark) | Airport authority grounds crews for salting/sanding
cms_content_angles: Slipped on ice at [airport_name] entrance | Fell in snow at [airport_name] parking lot | Who is responsible for icy sidewalks at [airport_name]
common_injuries: Hip fractures (most common in elderly) | Wrist fractures | Knee injuries | Spinal injuries | Head trauma from falls on ice
compensable_damages: Medical bills | Lost wages | Surgery costs | Physical therapy | Pain and suffering | Future care
contributing_environmental_factors: Active storm vs post-storm (legal distinction) | Black ice forming after freezing rain | Inadequate entrance matting saturated by heavy rain | Drainage failures creating puddles at building entries | Snow plow creating dangerous refreeze conditions
danger_note: Ice and snow on airport surfaces cause some of the most severe slip and fall injuries — hip fractures in elderly travelers are common
description: Slip and fall injuries in outdoor areas during rain, snow, and ice including airport entrances, covered walkways, and outdoor transportation areas.
evidence_notes: Document the exact location and condition with photos immediately — snow and ice melt quickly and the scene will change rapidly.
factors_increasing_value: Elderly victim with hip fracture | Black ice with no treatment | Prior fall at same location | Snow removal contractor clearly negligent
frequency_note: High frequency at northern airports in winter months; significant at all airports during rain events
high_risk_times: Winter storm events | Early morning freeze after rain | Sleet and freezing rain conditions | Spring thaw creating ice/slush mix
insurance_types_involved: Premises liability | Contractor liability | Government liability (where applicable)
key_evidence: Weather records for date/time of incident | Snow removal logs | De-icing application records | Photographs of condition | Incident report | Witness statements
liability_notes: Government immunity issues may affect claims against public airport authorities in some states. Weather conditions do not automatically absolve responsibility.
liable_party_defenses: Storm in progress doctrine (active storm relief) | Reasonable time to treat had not elapsed | Victim was wearing inappropriate footwear
local_authority_involved: Airport Authority / Snow Removal Contractor / Facility Manager
long_term_complications: Hip replacement surgery | Chronic mobility impairment | Long-term physical therapy
passenger_volume_note: Storm events cause both bad weather AND flight delays — delayed-flight passengers lingering create higher exposure to wet conditions
plaintiff_counter_strategies: Show storm had ended (eliminating storm-in-progress defense) | Prove inadequate inspection schedule after storm | Show prior incidents at same location
primary_airport_zones: Airport main entrances | Covered walkways between parking and terminal | Curbside pickup/dropoff | Jet bridge exterior (rain) | Outdoor terminal connections
primary_liable_parties: Airport authority | Snow removal contractor | Facility management company | Parking operator (for lot areas)
red_flags_for_attorneys: Storm had ended hours before fall (eliminates storm-in-progress defense) | Salt/sand application records show gap in coverage | Prior falls at same entrance during weather | Drainage system was known to be defective creating standing water
reportability: Photograph the exact spot IMMEDIATELY before conditions change — weather-related scenes change within minutes
secondary_airport_zones: Parking garage stairwells (water tracked in) | Crosswalks between buildings | Rental car return areas
secondary_liable_parties: De-icing chemical manufacturer (if product failed) | Building contractor (drainage design defect)
specific_floor_materials: Entrance tile (most dangerous when wet) | Covered walkway surfaces | Outdoor asphalt crosswalks | Parking garage stairwell surfaces (water tracked in)
statute_of_limitations_note: 1-3 years depending on state. Government airports may require Notice of Claim within 60-180 days.
terminal_zones_ranked: Airport main entrance doors (highest tracking) | Covered walkways from parking (gaps letting rain in) | Curbside pickup area (fully exposed) | Outdoor jetbridge stairs (rain/snow) | Crosswalks between buildings
venue_specific_hazards: entrance_mats: Inadequate or saturated entrance mats failing to absorb rain/snow || black_ice: Black ice formation on shaded walkways after freezing rain || snow_removal: Snow piled creating ice after partial melt/refreeze || covered_walkway: Water intrusion through gaps in covered walkways || outdoor_stairs: Iced over stair treads without adequate de-icing treatment
victim_demographics: Elderly passengers (most severe injury from falls on ice) | Passengers rushing to catch flights in storms | Travelers unfamiliar with black ice risk | Passengers with heavy luggage affecting balance

airport_businesses_involved: American Airlines | Delta Air Lines | United Airlines | Southwest Airlines | Alaska Airlines | JetBlue Airways | Frontier Airlines | Spirit Airlines | Airport authority (for terminal overnight facilities) | Marriott, Hilton, Hyatt, Wyndham (near-airport hotels) | Airport medical services | DOT (regulator of tarmac delay rules)
airport_size_note: Delay injuries occur at all airports but tarmac grounding incidents (worst category) primarily at large hubs with complex operations
average_annual_incidents_per_major_hub: Physical harm cases: 100-500+ per year during major weather events
business_categories: Airlines (all carriers) | Airport hotel operators | Airport authority | Airport food vendors during delays | Medical service providers at airports

contributing_environmental_factors: Weather causing system-wide groundings | Airline IT failures | Air traffic control system outages | Cascading delays from crew repositioning failures
danger_note: Tarmac delays without adequate water, medical attention, or restroom access create documented physical harm
description: Physical injuries and significant harm arising from extended airline delays, tarmac groundings, flight cancellations, and forced overnight stays in airports.
evidence_notes: Keep all receipts from delay-related expenses. File a DOT complaint immediately — creates official record.
factors_increasing_value: DOT tarmac rule violation | Medication deprivation causing serious harm | DVT with pulmonary embolism | Elderly or disabled passenger
frequency_note: Millions of passengers are affected by delays annually — physical harm claims are less common but legally significant
high_risk_times: During weather events causing system-wide delays | Holiday travel peak cancellations | Airline IT outages | Air traffic control ground stops
insurance_claim_type: Negligence / DOT violation / breach of contract
insurance_types_involved: Aviation liability | Travel insurance (consumer side) | General liability
key_evidence: Flight records showing delay duration | DOT tarmac delay reports | Medical records | Passenger communications from airline | Boarding passes | Hotel receipts from forced overnight
legal_standard: Negligence / DOT regulations / breach of contract
liability_notes: DOT's 3-hour domestic / 4-hour international tarmac delay rule creates a regulatory baseline. Physical harm beyond financial inconvenience opens tort liability.
liable_party_defenses: Delay was due to weather / safety | DOT rules were complied with | Physical harm pre-existed delay
local_authority_involved: Airline / DOT Office of Aviation Consumer Protection / Airport Authority
long_term_complications: DVT complications / pulmonary embolism | Exacerbated chronic conditions

plaintiff_counter_strategies: Show DOT tarmac rule was violated | Prove airline had option to return to gate and refused | Document medical harm with immediate treatment records
primary_airport_zones: Departure gates | Tarmac aircraft during groundings | Terminal overnight sleeping areas | Rebooking counter areas
primary_liable_parties: Airline (primary) | Airport authority (for terminal conditions)
red_flags_for_attorneys: Tarmac delay exceeded 3 hours domestic / 4 hours international | Passenger was denied water, restroom, or medical access | Medication was in checked bag stranded on aircraft | DOT complaint was filed and airline denied valid claim
reportability: File DOT complaint immediately (creates official timeline). Keep ALL receipts. Seek medical care same day if physical harm occurred
secondary_airport_zones: Airport hotels during forced stays | Airport medical clinics | Terminal seating areas during extended waits
secondary_liable_parties: Air traffic control (FAA — FTCA if delay was ATC-caused)
specific_floor_materials: Hard terminal gate seating areas | Aircraft floor during tarmac grounding
statute_of_limitations_note: 2-3 years for personal injury. Contract claims may have different periods.
